gaps: A Term/era estimate derived at ingest for old Miscellaneous Docket numbers (even a decade bucket) would let the predictor use era-specific base rates instead of inferring the era from the docket-number style.
A stats cut for the historical Miscellaneous (IFP) docket vs the paid docket would anchor P(granted) better than the all-SCOTUS aggregate, whose 78% 'other' mass dilutes the signal.
helpful: fedcourts paths to resolve the snapshot/event paths.
fedcourts stats for the resolved-SCOTUS disposition base rates (the calibration anchor).
schemas/prediction.schema.json as the exact output contract.
Prior claude-baseline petition-disposition predictions as a calibration and format anchor.
notes: Snapshot is a bare metadata stub (no docket entries, all disposition dates null), so the call rests on the '610, Misc' IFP-docket signal, the state-respondent caption, and corpus base rates. fedcourts query without a docket-number or era filter surfaced only loosely related old appeals, so it contributed little beyond confirming corpus composition. Did not open the linked opinion cluster (it is the outcome under evaluation) and did not use the CourtListener MCP for case facts.
